chunks.content:
A new election term, a new Minister for Auckland. The supercity that accounts for nearly 40% of the nation’s gross domestic product and workforce will have Minister Simeon Brown representing its interests in the halls of Parliament for the next three years, all going to plan.

The MP for Pakuranga’s Auckland portfolio will have him working alongside Mayor Wayne Brown and his council as well as countless other stakeholders to continue getting Tāmaki Makaurau through major economic and infrastructural challenges.

In an official briefing to the incoming minister prepared by the Ministry for Business, Innovation and Employment, handed over in November 2023 and released to the public this week, Brown received 16 pages of suggestions and advice on how to handle the role.

The briefing painted a picture of a struggling region that was too big to fail.

“Despite its strong macroeconomic performance, Auckland faces challenges,” it said. “Many places and communities have yet to substantially benefit from overall economic growth. The region's employment levels are at a record high. However, issues around employment quality and distribution impact wider social investment outcomes and living standards.

“In recent years, the region has faced challenges due to Covid-19, inflation, labour market pressures, and severe weather events. These have disrupted economic activities and impacted the region's economic sentiment. Some of these effects may be ongoing.”

The report referenced social, economic and health disparities, lagging productivity - below the national average - and issues with resilience, as the region faced growing by 500,000 people over the next three decades, which could further strain infrastructure.

“Notwithstanding these challenges, Auckland has enormous potential. Your appointment as Minister for Auckland enables coordinated Government support to address challenges that are unique to the region. This can help foster a productive and resilient economy that embraces technology and innovation, while stimulating economic growth for the success for Aucklanders and in turn New Zealand.”

A new election term, a new Minister for Auckland. The supercity that accounts for nearly 40% of the nation’s gross domestic product and workforce will have Minister Simeon Brown representing its interests in the halls of Parliament for the next three years, all going to plan.

The MP for Pakuranga’s Auckland portfolio will have him working alongside Mayor Wayne Brown and his council as well as countless other stakeholders to continue getting Tāmaki Makaurau through major economic and infrastructural challenges.

In an official briefing to the incoming minister prepared by the Ministry for Business, Innovation and Employment, handed over in November 2023 and released to the public this week, Brown received 16 pages of suggestions and advice on how to handle the role.

The briefing painted a picture of a struggling region that was too big to fail.

“Despite its strong macroeconomic performance, Auckland faces challenges,” it said. “Many places and communities have yet to substantially benefit from overall economic growth. The region's employment levels are at a record high. However, issues around employment quality and distribution impact wider social investment outcomes and living standards.

“In recent years, the region has faced challenges due to Covid-19, inflation, labour market pressures, and severe weather events. These have disrupted economic activities and impacted the region's economic sentiment. Some of these effects may be ongoing.”

The report referenced social, economic and health disparities, lagging productivity - below the national average - and issues with resilience, as the region faced growing by 500,000 people over the next three decades, which could further strain infrastructure.

“Notwithstanding these challenges, Auckland has enormous potential. Your appointment as Minister for Auckland enables coordinated Government support to address challenges that are unique to the region. This can help foster a productive and resilient economy that embraces technology and innovation, while stimulating economic growth for the success for Aucklanders and in turn New Zealand.”

chunks.content:
Said Heart of the City chief executive Viv Beck on Friday.“We need a minister who has a good holistic understanding of the needs of our region.”

Heart of the City specifically focuses on central Auckland, which represents some 20% of the region’s GDP.

A strong minister for Auckland would work as a “glue” between central and local government priorities, and work beyond the three year electoral cycle, said Beck.

She’d been able to speak to Brown since the election, and thought he was receptive.

Top of Beck’s wish-list is for Auckland’s transport and crime issues to be fixed. The new government deserved a chance, but change needed to come fast, she said.

“Obviously I'd rather say ‘growth’ and those sorts of initiatives because that's what we really need, but there are some fundamental issues we really have to get on top of urgently.

“It's been quite an extensive election period and I think some of these topics have been quite well canvassed, so we’d like to see some things happen quite quickly.“

Former Minister for Auckland, Michael Wood, established the Auckland Ministerial Advisory Group in March 2023, which met monthly and offered support on the post-flooding recovery in particular.

The 10-person committee is chaired by former deputy mayor of Auckland, Penny Hulse, and includes former leader of the National Party and current CEO of Auckland Business Chamber, Simon Bridges.

The briefing to Brown also included the task of deciding whether to continue with the group. Advice on this was redacted.

Another key element of the briefing was an outline of Auckland Mayor Wayne Brown’s proposal for a long-term Auckland deal that he set out before the 2023 election.

Said Beck, “Alignment between central and local government will be critical, so we actually have an agreed pipeline of large scale projects and how they'll be funded so we can avoid this sort of flip-flop stuff where this project gets worked on by one government and then it doesn't happen.

“And, you know, that's just sort of an ongoing cycle and I think we have to move right away from that.”

Former Minister for Auckland, Michael Wood, established the Auckland Ministerial Advisory Group in March 2023, which met monthly and offered support on the post-flooding recovery in particular.

The 10-person committee is chaired by former deputy mayor of Auckland, Penny Hulse, and includes former leader of the National Party and current CEO of Auckland Business Chamber, Simon Bridges.

Said Beck, “Alignment between central and local government will be critical, so we actually have an agreed pipeline of large scale projects and how they'll be funded so we can avoid this sort of flip-flop stuff where this project gets worked on by one government and then it doesn't happen.

chunks.content:
Also in the group is community paediatrician Dr Jin Russell, who focused on the needs of children and young people in Tāmaki Makaurau.

Years under Covid-19 lockdowns and major weather events in 2023 have put Aucklanders on the back foot, she said.

“We've got a need to provide healthy rhythms and to reduce disruption for our families, but also to bring about the kinds of initiatives which make it possible to enjoy raising a family here no matter what your income level is,“ Russell said.

“We need to give children stability, great services, a great schooling experience and great health care. Those are the things which make for really brilliant young people.

“There’s a role for the new incoming minister to listen deeply to community and what they’re experiencing on the ground through community leaders,” she said.

“It's pretty clear to me that the number of challenges that the Auckland region has faced are tremendous and there's a real desire from the group and from the minister to understand deeply what communities were going through.”
